meta.title: Schankian Operators — Research Roadmap
meta.description: The 8-phase research program from discovery to deployment
meta.author: Clawd & Peter

children.children: This roadmap organizes our Schankian operators research into eight phases, from initial discovery through real-system deployment. Each phase builds on the previous ones, though several can be pursued in parallel. The brainstorm entries (numbered from our working notes) feed into specific phases.

props.caption: The 8-phase pipeline from discovery to deployment
props.drawFunction: const phases = ['Discovery','Annotation','Formalization','Grounding','Planning','Composition','Validation','Deployment']; const colors = ['#3b82f6','#8b5cf6','#6366f1','#14b8a6','#f59e0b','#ec4899','#10b981','#ef4444']; const w=70, h=40, gap=10, startX=15, startY=50; phases.forEach((p,i) => { const x = startX + i*(w+gap); ctx.fillStyle = colors[i]; ctx.beginPath(); ctx.roundRect(x, startY, w, h, 8); ctx.fill(); ctx.fillStyle = '#fff'; ctx.font = 'bold 9px sans-serif'; ctx.textAlign = 'center'; ctx.fillText(p, x+w/2, startY+h/2+3); if(i < phases.length-1) { ctx.fillStyle = '#94a3b8'; ctx.beginPath(); ctx.moveTo(x+w+2, startY+h/2); ctx.lineTo(x+w+gap-2, startY+h/2-4); ctx.lineTo(x+w+gap-2, startY+h/2+4); ctx.fill(); } });

props.caption: Dependency web — all phases orbit the core Operators concept
props.drawFunction: const labels = ['Discovery','Annotation','Formalization','Grounding','Planning','Composition','Validation','Deployment']; const colors = ['#3b82f6','#8b5cf6','#6366f1','#14b8a6','#f59e0b','#ec4899','#10b981','#ef4444']; const cx=250, cy=250, R=170, r=40, cr=50; ctx.fillStyle='#4f46e5'; ctx.beginPath(); ctx.arc(cx,cy,cr,0,Math.PI*2); ctx.fill(); ctx.fillStyle='#fff'; ctx.font='bold 13px sans-serif'; ctx.textAlign='center'; ctx.fillText('Operators',cx,cy+5); labels.forEach((l,i)=>{ const a = (i/labels.length)*Math.PI*2 - Math.PI/2; const x=cx+R*Math.cos(a), y=cy+R*Math.sin(a); ctx.strokeStyle=colors[i]; ctx.lineWidth=2; ctx.beginPath(); ctx.moveTo(cx+cr*Math.cos(a),cy+cr*Math.sin(a)); ctx.lineTo(x-r*Math.cos(a),y-r*Math.sin(a)); ctx.stroke(); ctx.fillStyle=colors[i]; ctx.beginPath(); ctx.arc(x,y,r,0,Math.PI*2); ctx.fill(); ctx.fillStyle='#fff'; ctx.font='bold 10px sans-serif'; ctx.fillText(l,x,y+4); });

children.children: The foundation: empirically demonstrating that semantic operators exist as recoverable structure in neural network representations. We probe trained models, mine sparse autoencoders, apply semantic role labeling, search for eigenoperators in weight matrices, and catalog attention motifs that correspond to primitive actions.
#20 — Probing classifiers for operator representations
#25 — SAE mining for operator-like features
#26 — Semantic role labeling as operator extraction
#36 — Eigenoperators from weight decomposition
#41 — Attention motifs as primitive action signatures
Key insight: Operators aren't just theoretical — they leave measurable fingerprints in trained neural networks. The SAE work (#25) has already shown promising clusters.

Before we can train models to use operators, we need labeled data. This phase builds the annotation infrastructure: connecting to ATOMIC's 880K commonsense tuples, extending to multimodal grounding, using near-miss contrasts for boundary sharpening, and enriching with epistemic status markers.
Annotation system — core infrastructure for operator labeling
#14 — ATOMIC relations as operator decomposition
#19 — Multimodal grounding of operators
#35 — Near-miss contrasts for operator boundaries
#50 — Epistemic status markers on operator annotations
Key insight: ATOMIC already encodes operator-like structure across 9 relation types. We're not starting from scratch — we're re-interpreting existing resources through an operator lens.

Operators need rigorous mathematical foundations. The core thesis is that semantic operators form a non-commutative algebra (order matters: 'grasp then release' ≠ 'release then grasp'). We explore Lie algebra structure for continuous operator families, category theory for compositional semantics, type systems for safety, Galois connections for abstraction levels, and sheaf theory for local-to-global coherence.
Core thesis — Non-commutative algebra of semantic operators
#37 — Lie algebra structure of operator families
#38 — Category theory for operator composition
#40 — Type systems for operator safety
#49 — Galois connections between abstraction levels
#53 — Sheaf theory for local-global coherence
Key insight: Non-commutativity is the linchpin. If operators commuted, they'd be just features. Non-commutativity is what makes them actions — and what makes the math hard but rewarding.

Update (2026-02-12): The formalization now includes full rotation matrices as operators on concatenated participant embeddings. This connects to category theory (#38): operators are morphisms in a category where objects are multi-entity state tuples, and the matrix IS the morphism.

Theory must become computation. This phase turns operators into embeddings that neural networks can actually use. The architecture has evolved from RotatE-style diagonal rotations to full rotation matrices on concatenated participant embeddings — capturing cross-entity feature interactions that diagonal operations miss.
#55 — Full rotation matrices on concatenated participants (PRIMARY ARCHITECTURE)
#56 — Rate-distortion bounds on operator compression
RotatE experiments — diagonal rotation embeddings (Phase 1 proof of concept)
#11 — Disentangled operator representations
#39 — Information bottleneck for operator compression
#44 — Periodic table of semantic operators
#45 — MDL complexity measures for operator parsimony
#54 — DreamCoder-style library learning for operator discovery

Architecture pivot (2026-02-12): RotatE-style diagonal rotations treat each embedding dimension independently — they can't express feature INTERACTIONS like 'smart AND arrogant.' Full rotation matrices on concatenated participant embeddings solve this: the operator transforms [agent|object|recipient] as one vector, with cross-entity feature mixing. The flat array → Bloom filter → bitmask → rotation → full matrix derivation (Brainstorm #55) shows this is the natural endpoint.

Operators transform states, so we need state machines. This phase builds differentiable planning systems: script-based operator sequences, differentiable state machines, forward-inverse model pairs, differentiable STRIPS planning, aspectual operators (start/continue/finish), and ADSR envelopes for operator dynamics over time.
#13 — Scripts as canonical operator sequences
#23 — Differentiable state machines for operator chains
#27 — Forward-inverse model pairs
#28 — Differentiable STRIPS planning
#30 — Aspectual operators (start/continue/finish)
#43 — ADSR envelopes for operator dynamics
Key insight: Classical AI planning (STRIPS) and neural sequence modeling are two sides of the same coin. Differentiable state machines bridge them — operators become the shared vocabulary.

Individual operators are atoms; stories and explanations are molecules. This phase tackles how operators compose into larger structures: hierarchical nesting, force dynamics for causal chains, conservation laws constraining valid compositions, renormalization for multi-scale coherence, and operadic composition for formal algebraic structure.
#9 — Hierarchical operator nesting
#10 — Force dynamics for causal chains
#31 — Conservation laws for operator compositions
#47 — Renormalization for multi-scale coherence
#48 — Operadic composition algebra

Key insight: Composition isn't free — it's constrained by conservation laws. You can't create energy from nothing, and you can't compose operators that violate semantic invariants. These constraints are features, not bugs.

Update (2026-02-12): Full rotation matrices compose naturally via matrix multiplication. An ATRANS followed by a PTRANS is just W_PTRANS · W_ATRANS applied to the concatenated state. This is more elegant than composing diagonal rotations, which can only compose element-wise.

Claims need evidence. This phase designs experiments to test whether operator-based representations actually improve on baselines: counterfactual probing, contrastive evaluation, dual-space consistency checks, topological data analysis for representation geometry, and simulation environments for grounded testing.
#3 — Counterfactual probing experiments
#16 — Contrastive evaluation benchmarks
#42 — Dual-space consistency checks
#46 — TDA for representation geometry
#12 — Simulation environments for grounded testing
Key insight: The strongest test is counterfactual — if we ablate an operator, does the model lose exactly the capability we predict? Clean causal evidence beats correlation.

The endgame: operators in production. This phase connects operators to real applications: curriculum learning that teaches operators in pedagogically optimal order, LLM interfaces that expose operator structure, affordance-based interaction, semantic information retrieval, and climbing Pearl's causal ladder from association through intervention to counterfactual reasoning.
#18 — Curriculum learning for operator acquisition
#24 — LLM interface exposing operator structure
#29 — Affordance-based operator interaction
#32 — Semantic information retrieval via operators
#52 — Pearl's causal ladder integration

Key insight: The killer app may be semantic IR — searching by what happened (operator) rather than what was said (keywords). 'Find all events where someone gained control of a resource' is an operator query.

props.title: Phase 9 — Mechanistic Interpretability: The Literary Titles

children.children: A parallel track that emerged from asking: if operators exist as structure in neural networks (Phase 1), can we find the specific circuits that implement them? This phase applies mechanistic interpretability to trace how transformers detect, classify, and exploit semantic primitives — using literary-themed papers that each isolate one aspect of the pipeline.

Key connection: Phase 1 asks 'do operators leave fingerprints?' Phase 9 asks 'what are the circuits that produce those fingerprints?' Together they provide evidence from both directions — top-down (probing) and bottom-up (circuit tracing).
The papers are ordered as a pipeline: detection → interpretation → specialization → structure mapping → unification.

Paper 1 ✅ — "The Anxiety of Influence: Bloom Filters in Transformer Attention Heads" — DRAFT COMPLETE. Identifies 4 heads in GPT-2 that implement Bloom filter membership testing ('have I seen this token before?'). Zero false negatives, characteristic false positives, capacity curve fits theory at R²=0.99. Cross-validated on 4 models. Ablation confirms functional specificity. Target: arXiv → NeurIPS 2026.

Paper 2 🔬 — "The Second Time Around: How Transformers Turn Repetition into Meaning" — EXPERIMENTS RUNNING. Traces what happens AFTER Bloom filter detection: the circuit from membership test to semantic interpretation. Key finding so far: representations of 1st and 2nd occurrences maximally diverge at layers 3-6 (the 'gap layers' between Bloom detection and induction head exploitation).

Paper 3 📋 — "Too Hip, Gotta Go: Idiom Recognition as an Early Exit Signal" — PLANNED. When to stop disambiguating: frozen phrases as processing shortcuts. Connects to Bloom heads (membership check → early exit).

Paper 4 📋 — "Curious Hair: Reference Detection as a Tool for Fostering Context" — PLANNED. Coreference and literary allusion as context-building mechanisms (DFW's maximalist reference density). Two-pass architecture: detection → enriched context.
Paper 5 📅 — "It's Like a Simile: Analogy Recognition and Application" — LATER. Structure-mapping (Gentner) via Schankian operators. Same operator transforms A→B and C→D = analogy detected.

Paper 6 🏛️ — "The Best of All Possible Worlds: Stack-Ranking Semantic Contexts as Conjectured World Models" — CAPSTONE. Montague/Leibniz: inference as navigating possible worlds. Residual stream states decompose into ranked interpretations. Unifies everything: Bloom filters = world pruning, repetition = world updating, idioms = world collapse, analogy = world mapping.

Cross-connections: Paper 1's Bloom filter heads relate to Phase 1's attention motifs (#41). Paper 2's repetition-as-meaning connects to Phase 6's hierarchical composition — repetition detection is a primitive that higher-level circuits compose into coreference, emphasis, and pattern completion. Paper 5 (analogy) directly instantiates Phase 3's operator algebra — if the same operator transforms two different input pairs, that IS analogy. Paper 6 (possible worlds) provides the theoretical umbrella that connects Phases 3-8 to mechanistic evidence.

props.caption: The Literary Titles pipeline: from detection to unification
props.drawFunction:
const papers = [{n:'Anxiety of
Influence',s:'done',sub:'Detection'},{n:'Second Time
Around',s:'active',sub:'Interpretation'},{n:'Too Hip
Gotta Go',s:'planned',sub:'Specialization'},{n:'Curious
Hair',s:'planned',sub:'Context'},{n:'Like a
Simile',s:'planned',sub:'Structure'},{n:'Best of All
Worlds',s:'planned',sub:'Unification'}]; const colors = {done:'#22c55e',active:'#f59e0b',planned:'#94a3b8'}; const w=95, h=55, gap=12, y=45; papers.forEach((p,i)=>{ const x=8+i*(w+gap); ctx.fillStyle=colors[p.s]; ctx.globalAlpha=p.s==='planned'?0.4:1; ctx.beginPath(); ctx.roundRect(x,y,w,h,8); ctx.fill(); ctx.globalAlpha=1; ctx.fillStyle='#fff'; ctx.font='bold 9px sans-serif'; ctx.textAlign='center'; const lines=p.n.split('
'); ctx.fillText(lines[0],x+w/2,y+h/2-4); if(lines[1]) ctx.fillText(lines[1],x+w/2,y+h/2+8); ctx.fillStyle=colors[p.s]; ctx.globalAlpha=p.s==='planned'?0.5:0.8; ctx.font='italic 9px sans-serif'; ctx.fillText(p.sub,x+w/2,y+h+14); ctx.globalAlpha=1; if(i<papers.length-1){ctx.fillStyle='#64748b';ctx.beginPath();ctx.moveTo(x+w+2,y+h/2);ctx.lineTo(x+w+gap-2,y+h/2-4);ctx.lineTo(x+w+gap-2,y+h/2+4);ctx.fill();} }); ctx.font='11px sans-serif'; ctx.fillStyle='#22c55e'; ctx.fillText('✅ Draft',120,25); ctx.fillStyle='#f59e0b'; ctx.fillText('🔬 Active',300,25); ctx.fillStyle='#94a3b8'; ctx.fillText('📋 Planned',500,25);

children.children: Phase 1 (Discovery) has produced validated results — SAE mining and probing experiments show clear operator-like structure. Phases 2 (Annotation) and 3 (Formalization) are actively in progress. Phase 9 (Mechanistic Interpretability) has launched in parallel: Paper 1 ('The Anxiety of Influence') has a complete draft with 6 experiments, statistical hardening, and 7 publication-quality figures. Paper 2 ('The Second Time Around') has initial experiments running. Phases 4–8 are planned.

props.caption: Phase status: ✅ validated, 🔄 active, 📋 planned
props.drawFunction: const phases = [{n:'1 Discovery',s:'done'},{n:'2 Annotation',s:'active'},{n:'3 Formal',s:'active'},{n:'4 Ground',s:'planned'},{n:'5 Planning',s:'planned'},{n:'6 Compose',s:'planned'},{n:'7 Validate',s:'planned'},{n:'8 Deploy',s:'planned'},{n:'9 MechInterp',s:'active'}]; const colors = {done:'#22c55e',active:'#f59e0b',planned:'#94a3b8'}; const w=58, h=50, gap=5, y=35; phases.forEach((p,i)=>{ const x=6+i*(w+gap); ctx.fillStyle=colors[p.s]; ctx.globalAlpha=p.s==='planned'?0.4:1; ctx.beginPath(); ctx.roundRect(x,y,w,h,6); ctx.fill(); ctx.globalAlpha=1; ctx.fillStyle='#fff'; ctx.font='bold 8px sans-serif'; ctx.textAlign='center'; const lines=p.n.split(' '); ctx.fillText(lines[0],x+w/2,y+h/2-2); if(lines[1]) ctx.fillText(lines[1],x+w/2,y+h/2+10); }); ctx.font='11px sans-serif'; ctx.fillStyle='#22c55e'; ctx.fillText('✅ Validated',80,20); ctx.fillStyle='#f59e0b'; ctx.fillText('🔄 Active',250,20); ctx.fillStyle='#94a3b8'; ctx.fillText('📋 Planned',430,20);

children.children: Next milestones: (1) Post 'Anxiety of Influence' to arXiv and submit to NeurIPS 2026. (2) Complete 'Second Time Around' experiments on repetition-to-meaning circuits. (3) Implement full rotation matrix experiments (Phase 4). (4) Publish multi-entity annotation schema (Phase 2).
